If they succeed within 3-5 attempts, the difficulty is appropriate and the issue may be frustration from long death-to-retry time rather than actual difficulty. Design change: place a checkpoint closer to the challenge so retries are faster. The cardinal rule: the player is always right about their experience but rarely right about the solution.

rubric.key: Explains why 'too hard' is insufficient (symptom vs diagnosis)
Three specific follow-up observations/questions that target different root causes
Appropriate design change for each identified root cause

questions.question: Explain how ResourceLoader.load_threaded_request() works in Godot 4 for asynchronous level loading. Why is this important for player experience? Describe the complete flow from the player pressing 'Next Level' to the new level being fully loaded and visible.
questions.answer: ResourceLoader.load_threaded_request(path) starts loading a resource on a background thread without blocking the main thread. The main thread can continue rendering frames (showing a loading screen, animation, tips) while the level loads. Check progress with ResourceLoader.load_threaded_get_status(path) which returns THREAD_LOAD_IN_PROGRESS, THREAD_LOAD_LOADED, or THREAD_LOAD_FAILED. Retrieve the loaded resource with ResourceLoader.load_threaded_get(path) once status is LOADED. This is important because synchronous loading with load() or preload() freezes the game for the entire load duration -- on slower machines or for large levels, this can mean seconds of frozen screen, which players perceive as a crash. Complete flow: (1) Player presses Next Level, (2) TransitionManager plays fade-to-black animation (0.3s), (3) On fade complete, show loading screen with progress bar and tip text, (4) Call ResourceLoader.load_threaded_request(next_level_path), (5) In _process(), poll load_threaded_get_status() and update progress bar, (6) When status is LOADED, call get_tree().change_scene_to_packed(load_threaded_get(path)), (7) New scene _ready() initializes, (8) TransitionManager plays fade-from-black, (9) Player sees new level.

rubric.key: Correctly explains threaded loading API (request, get_status, get)
Explains why synchronous loading degrades player experience
Complete flow from button press to level visible with transition manager

questions.question: You are preparing your game for release on itch.io with both an HTML5 web build and a Windows downloadable build. List three platform-specific issues you must address for the HTML5 build that do not apply to the Windows build, and explain the solution for each.
questions.answer: Three HTML5-specific issues: (1) Audio autoplay restrictions: Web browsers block audio playback until the user has interacted with the page (clicked or tapped). Solution: add a 'Click to Start' splash screen before the game begins that satisfies the browser's user interaction requirement, or use AudioContext.resume() on first user input. (2) SharedArrayBuffer and COOP/COEP headers: Godot 4 HTML5 exports require SharedArrayBuffer for threading, which requires Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy and Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy HTTP headers. Solution: configure the itch.io page to use the SharedArrayBuffer option, or if self-hosting, set the COOP: same-origin and COEP: require-corp headers on the web server. (3) Save file persistence: the user:// directory in HTML5 uses IndexedDB, which browsers may clear during cache cleanup, and file sizes are limited. Solution: implement smaller save files (JSON instead of binary Resource), warn players that browser data clearing may affect saves, and consider offering save file export/import functionality.

rubric.key: Identifies audio autoplay restriction with correct solution
Identifies SharedArrayBuffer/CORS header requirement with solution
Identifies save persistence limitation with practical mitigation

major_assignments.title: 2D Platformer Prototype
major_assignments.description: Build a playable 2D platformer level in Godot 4 featuring a player character with polished movement, animated sprites driven by a state machine, tile-based level design with autotiling, tuned physics with coyote time and input buffering, a parallax background camera system, and particle effects for player feedback. The level must be completable in 2-3 minutes and demonstrate mastery of core 2D development techniques covered in weeks 1-6.
objectives: Implement responsive player movement with a documented animation state machine
Design a tile-based level with autotiling, collision layers, and one-way platforms
Apply game feel principles: coyote time, input buffering, variable jump height, screen shake
Create a camera system with smooth following, look-ahead, and parallax scrolling
Add particle effects for at least 3 player actions (jump dust, landing impact, direction change)
deliverables: Godot 4 project with all source files and assets committed to Git
Level design document with annotated map showing intended player path and pacing
Technical write-up (2-3 pages) explaining state machine design, physics tuning decisions, and camera configuration

criteria: Smooth, responsive movement with appropriate acceleration and deceleration curves
Animation state machine with correct transitions for all movement states (idle, run, jump_rise, jump_fall, land)
Coyote time, input buffering, and variable jump height implemented and tuned to appropriate values
Scene tree is well-organized with descriptive node names and proper component separation
TileMap with autotiling configured correctly for seamless terrain
Camera follows smoothly with look-ahead and parallax background (at least 2 layers)
Particle effects provide clear feedback for player actions
Consistent pixel art style at chosen resolution with no sub-pixel rendering artifacts
Level teaches mechanics through design rather than text instructions
Appropriate difficulty curve over 2-3 minutes of gameplay
At least one hazard type and one collectible type with visual/audio feedback
Screen shake or camera effects on significant impacts
Level design document includes annotated map with pacing analysis
Technical write-up explains state machine design decisions with diagrams
Physics tuning values are documented with rationale for each
GDScript follows style guide with type hints and comments on non-obvious logic
Polished, playable platformer with responsive controls and clear state machine
Well-designed level that teaches through play with appropriate pacing
Professional camera work, parallax, and particle effects
Clean, well-documented codebase with thorough design documentation

major_assignments.title: Audio and Save System Integration
major_assignments.description: Extend your platformer prototype with a complete audio pipeline (music, SFX, ambient sound with bus routing), a save/load system that persists player progress between sessions, and a functional UI layer including HUD, pause menu, and settings screen. This assignment focuses on the support systems that transform a gameplay prototype into a complete player experience.
objectives: Implement an AudioManager with bus routing, sound variation, and music crossfading
Build a save/load system with versioned save data and error recovery
Create a HUD displaying health, score, and collectible count with smooth animations
Design a pause menu with keyboard/gamepad navigation and a settings screen for volume control
deliverables: Updated Godot 4 project with audio, save, and UI systems integrated
Exported builds (HTML5 and Windows) demonstrating save persistence across restarts
Audio design document listing all sound events, bus routing, and effect parameters
System architecture diagram showing how AudioManager, SaveManager, and UI interact with game systems

criteria: AudioManager autoload with at least 3 buses (Music, SFX, Ambient) and appropriate effects
Sound variation (pitch randomization, multiple samples) for frequently played effects
Music crossfading between at least 2 tracks triggered by gameplay state
Audio pooling prevents frame hitches from runtime AudioStreamPlayer allocation
SaveManager uses versioned Custom Resources with migration support
Save data includes player position, collected items, and current level
Error handling for first-play (no save file), corrupted data, and version mismatches
Autosave triggers at level transitions or checkpoints
HUD displays health (animated bar), score, and collectibles in a CanvasLayer
Pause menu with resume, settings, and quit options navigable by keyboard and gamepad
Settings screen controls volume per bus with changes that persist in save data
Screen transitions (fade-to-black) between menu and game scenes
Audio design document is thorough and matches implementation
System architecture diagram clearly shows manager interactions
Code is documented with type hints and explanatory comments
Save data format is documented with version history
Professional audio pipeline with bus routing, variation, and crossfading
Robust save/load system with versioning and error recovery
Functional UI with animated HUD and navigable menus
Clean documentation and system architecture

major_assignments.title: Complete 2D Game Vertical Slice
major_assignments.description: Expand your platformer into a vertical slice featuring at least 3 playable levels with distinct themes, 2+ enemy types with AI state machines, a hitbox/hurtbox combat system with juice effects (hit stop, screen shake, particles), a level select screen with star ratings, and playtesting documentation showing iterative design improvements. The vertical slice must represent a polished, shippable sample of a larger game.
objectives: Implement at least 2 enemy types with distinct AI state machines and attack patterns
Build a combat system with hitbox/hurtbox separation, invincibility frames, and juice effects
Design 3+ levels with distinct visual themes, escalating difficulty, and star rating evaluation
Conduct 3 playtesting sessions with documentation of observations and design changes
Create a level select screen and level transition system with threaded loading
deliverables: Complete Godot 4 project with all source files, assets, and Git history
Exported builds: HTML5 and Windows, release-quality
Game design document covering enemy behaviors, level designs, and combat system
Playtest report: 3 sessions with observation logs, feedback triage, and documented design changes
Postmortem analysis (2-3 pages): what went well, what went wrong, lessons learned

criteria: At least 2 enemy types with distinct AI state machines and readable attack patterns
Hitbox/hurtbox combat with invincibility frames, knockback, and damage feedback
Hit stop, screen shake, particles, and sprite flash create satisfying combat feel
Level select screen displays lock state and star ratings from save data
3+ levels with distinct visual themes and tileset variations
Difficulty progression across levels is intentional and playtested
Level transitions are smooth with loading screens or fade effects
Visual consistency across all levels, enemies, and UI elements
Game runs at stable 60fps with no hitches during gameplay or transitions
Memory usage is stable across level changes (no leaks from unfired nodes)
Save/load works correctly across all levels and game states
All systems (audio, save, UI, AI, combat) work together without conflicts
Game design document accurately describes all implemented systems
Playtest reports show real observations from 3 different testers with specific feedback
Design changes are documented with before/after comparisons
Postmortem demonstrates critical self-reflection and professional development insight
Feature-complete vertical slice with polished combat, multiple levels, and enemy variety
All game systems integrated and working reliably together
Playtesting evidence shows iterative design improvement
Professional documentation including design doc, playtest reports, and postmortem

weekly_quizzes.description: Five quizzes (weeks 3, 5, 8, 11, 14) assessing engine knowledge, design patterns, and technical concepts

lab_assignments.description: Weekly hands-on lab exercises building specific game systems with graded submissions

major_assignments.description: Three major projects: Platformer Prototype (150pts), Audio/Save Integration (120pts), Vertical Slice (200pts)

midterm_examination.description: Midterm practical examination: timed implementation of a game system from specification

final_project.description: Final build presentation with live demonstration, postmortem analysis, and peer evaluation

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all lab sessions and playtesting days. More than 2 unexcused absences from labs may result in a full letter grade reduction. Playtesting sessions cannot be made up.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments are penalized 10% per day for up to 5 days. Builds that do not compile or run receive an automatic 50% penalty. Test your exports on a clean machine before submitting.
course_policies.collaboration: Individual projects must be your own code and assets. You may use free assets from itch.io or OpenGameArt with proper attribution in a CREDITS file. Sharing code solutions between students is prohibited. Peer playtesting and feedback are encouraged and graded.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All code must be written by you or properly attributed. Using AI code generation tools is permitted for boilerplate only (project setup, file I/O) and must be disclosed in your submission. Submitting tutorial code without substantial modification is plagiarism.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students must have a computer capable of running Godot 4.2+ (Windows, macOS, or Linux). A dedicated GPU is recommended but not required for 2D work. All required software (Godot, Aseprite trial/LibreSprite, Git) is free or open source.
